stories.title: Show HN: Scan your AI agents for dangerous capabilities
stories.paragraph: MakerChecker is an open-source tool released on GitHub that scans AI agents for potentially dangerous capabilities before deployment. The project addresses growing concern among practitioners that agentic systems can acquire or expose unintended abilities — such as file system access or network calls — that were not explicitly granted. The tool works by analyzing agent configurations and tool bindings against a set of capability risk rules, giving developers a pre-deployment audit step.

stories.title: Defeat air-gapped systems by exfiltrating data using Apple Find My network
stories.paragraph: A researcher published hzgl-air-bridge, a proof-of-concept tool that exfiltrates data from air-gapped systems by encoding it into Bluetooth Low Energy advertisements that are picked up and relayed by the Apple Find My network. The technique exploits the fact that Apple devices passively relay Find My location packets from unknown devices, turning any nearby Apple hardware into an unwitting data relay. The project demonstrates a covert channel that requires no internet connection on the target machine and no special hardware beyond a Bluetooth-capable device.

stories.title: The AI Marketing Backlash: Why 'AI-First' Brands Are Starting to Fall Flat
stories.paragraph: A piece from Breef argues that consumer and business sentiment is turning against companies that lead their branding with AI as a primary value proposition, as the label has become associated with cost-cutting and depersonalization rather than quality. The article cites cases where companies repositioned products as 'AI-first' only to see customer trust erode, particularly in service-oriented sectors. The core argument is that AI is increasingly viewed by buyers as a means to an end rather than a feature worth advertising.

stories.title: The AI Big Crunch Is Starting
stories.paragraph: Security professional Tony Northrup argues in a Medium post that the AI industry is entering a consolidation phase in which weaker players will fail and investment will concentrate among a small number of dominant providers. The piece draws on historical parallels with the dot-com bust, suggesting that current valuations in the AI sector are disconnected from demonstrated revenue and that a correction is underway. Northrup points to rising inference costs and commoditization of base models as structural pressures squeezing margins for mid-tier vendors.

stories.title: When AI Costs More Than the Engineer
stories.paragraph: Venture capitalist Tom Tunguz published an analysis projecting that AI API and tooling costs at many companies could exceed the fully-loaded cost of the engineers those tools were meant to augment by 2029. The post models spending trajectories based on current token pricing and observed enterprise adoption curves, noting that some teams are already past breakeven on individual high-frequency use cases. Tunguz frames this not as a reason to stop adoption but as a prompt to measure productivity gains rigorously rather than assuming positive ROI.

stories.title: ByteDance and Alibaba to disable humanlike AI custom agents as new rules loom
stories.paragraph: ByteDance and Alibaba have announced plans to disable features that allow users to create custom AI agents with humanlike personas, citing forthcoming Chinese government regulations on synthetic personas and AI impersonation. The move affects products including ByteDance's Doubao and Alibaba's Tongyi platforms, where users had been able to configure agents with custom names, voices, and personalities. Chinese regulators have signaled that AI systems presenting themselves as human without clear disclosure will face legal restrictions, prompting preemptive compliance steps from the two companies.

stories.title: Al Vigier: Canada's AI strategy shouldn't include secret Palantir bills
stories.paragraph: Writing in The Line, Al Vigier argues that the Canadian government's AI procurement strategy lacks transparency, specifically criticizing undisclosed contracts with Palantir Technologies for data analytics and AI services. Vigier contends that the absence of public disclosure around these contracts makes meaningful democratic oversight of AI adoption in government impossible. The piece calls for mandatory public reporting on AI vendor contracts above a threshold cost, citing analogous disclosure requirements in other OECD countries.

stories.title: I'm just so bored of AI
stories.paragraph: Terence Eden published a personal essay describing fatigue with the current AI media and product cycle, arguing that the volume of AI-adjacent announcements has made it difficult to distinguish meaningful developments from noise. Eden notes that nearly every consumer product now carries an AI feature label, diluting the signal for practitioners trying to track genuinely useful advances. The post resonated widely on Hacker News, accumulating substantial discussion about the gap between AI marketing and day-to-day utility.

stories.title: People Who Will Thrive in the AI Age
stories.paragraph: The Atlantic published an opinion piece examining which professional and personal traits correlate with success in a labor market being reshaped by AI tools, drawing on interviews with researchers at OpenAI and Anthropic. The article identifies comfort with iterative, prompt-driven workflows and domain expertise as more durable than general coding or writing skills that AI can replicate at scale. It also notes that roles requiring physical presence, legal accountability, or long-term relationship management show less near-term displacement.

stories.title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI
stories.paragraph: A paper posted to arXiv under the title 'The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI' provides a structured survey of architectures, evaluation methods, and failure modes for autonomous AI agent systems. The authors organize the field around four core components — perception, memory, planning, and action — and review over 200 papers published through mid-2026. Notably, the survey dedicates a section to alignment and safety considerations specific to multi-agent settings, where coordination failures can compound individual model errors.

stories.title: New AI tutor achieves 0.71-1.30 SD effect size in Dartmouth course [pdf]
stories.paragraph: A study presented at a workshop on intelligent textbooks reports that an AI tutoring system deployed in a Dartmouth course achieved a learning effect size of 0.71 to 1.30 standard deviations compared to a control condition. Effect sizes in this range are considered large in educational research, where human one-on-one tutoring has historically been benchmarked at around 2 standard deviations above conventional classroom instruction in Bloom's foundational work. The paper contributes to an ongoing body of evidence examining whether AI-driven tutoring can meaningfully close the gap with human instruction at scale.

stories.title: sqlite-utils 4.0rc3
stories.paragraph: Simon Willison released sqlite-utils 4.0rc3, the third release candidate for the major version of his Python library for manipulating SQLite databases from the command line and from code. The most significant new feature in this candidate is support for introspecting and creating compound foreign keys, which introduces a subtle breaking change to the table.foreign_keys API. Willison noted that the changelog since rc2 expanded substantially because he worked through a backlog of issues and pull requests with assistance from Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.5.

stories.title: Real Linux. In a browser tab. No install. No server. No Docker
stories.paragraph: LinuxOnTab is a project that runs a full Linux environment entirely in a browser tab using WebAssembly, requiring no server-side compute, no Docker container, and no local installation. The environment boots from a compressed filesystem image loaded into the browser and runs processes through a WASM-compiled kernel interface. This approach is notable for enabling reproducible Linux environments in air-gapped or locked-down machines where installing software is not permitted.

stories.title: New Microsoft 365 pricing live, some products up by 42% due to AI
stories.paragraph: Microsoft has raised prices on several Microsoft 365 plans, with some products seeing increases of up to 42%, with the company citing continuous AI-driven innovation as justification. The hikes are closely tied to the bundling of Copilot features, which critics have labeled an effective 'AI tax' on business customers who may not want or use the AI tools. The move is notable because it signals a broader industry trend of AI features being used to justify subscription price increases rather than being offered as optional add-ons.

stories.title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven't progressed enough
stories.paragraph: Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg told staff internally that AI agents have not advanced as quickly as he had anticipated, according to a report from TechCrunch. The admission is notable given Meta's significant public investment in AI development and Zuckerberg's previous statements positioning autonomous agents as a near-term priority for the company. The comments suggest a gap between the pace of internal expectations and actual capability development in agentic AI systems.

stories.title: Tripadvisor AI summaries give glowing reviews to dangerous hotels
stories.paragraph: A consumer watchdog investigation found that Tripadvisor's AI-generated review summaries produced positive descriptions of hotels that had received serious safety complaints from users. The findings raise concerns about AI summarization systems selectively emphasizing favorable content while omitting or downweighting negative signals, even when those signals relate to guest safety. The case illustrates a concrete harm from deploying generative summarization in contexts where accuracy and completeness are critical for consumer decision-making.

stories.title: Tell HN: don't trust Bigco AI agents with AI research IP
stories.paragraph: A Hacker News user raised concerns about sharing proprietary AI research with large AI service providers such as Claude Code or ChatGPT/Codex, arguing that companies like Anthropic and OpenAI have access to the intellectual property of millions of researchers and startups who are in direct competition with them. The post highlights the tension between the utility of AI coding assistants and the potential for training data or query logs to be used in ways that benefit the model provider at the expense of users. The concern centers on whether AI companies, as commercial entities, might use submitted data to gain competitive advantages, either directly through training or indirectly through product development.

stories.title: A sociotechnical threat model for AI-driven smart home devices
stories.paragraph: A paper posted to arXiv proposes a sociotechnical threat model specifically for AI-driven smart home devices, addressing risks that go beyond traditional cybersecurity frameworks to include social and behavioral manipulation vectors. The work is notable for framing smart home AI not just as a technical attack surface but as a system embedded in domestic social contexts, where threat actors could exploit behavioral patterns, household routines, or trust relationships. The model aims to give researchers and designers a structured vocabulary for identifying and mitigating harms that emerge from the intersection of AI capabilities and intimate home environments.

stories.title: The Mental Models I Use to Work with AI
stories.paragraph: A Substack post outlines a set of practical mental models the author uses when working with AI tools, framing AI interaction as a skill that benefits from deliberate conceptual frameworks rather than trial-and-error prompting. The piece covers approaches such as treating AI outputs as drafts requiring human judgment rather than finished products, and thinking about AI as a collaborator with particular strengths and failure modes. While opinion-driven, the post reflects a growing practitioner conversation about how to develop systematic habits around AI-assisted work.

stories.title: The AI Compass Quiz
stories.paragraph: A developer published a short web-based quiz called the AI Compass, designed to map users' views on AI along multiple dimensions, drawing on the format of political compass-style assessments. The tool prompts respondents with statements about AI development, safety, and deployment, then plots their positions on a two-axis chart. It serves as a lightweight tool for surfacing and visualizing variation in AI-related attitudes among practitioners and the general public.

stories.title: Ask HN: Why about a third of the submissions become dead in mere minutes?
stories.paragraph: A Hacker News user observed that roughly one-third of new submissions on the site are marked dead within minutes of posting, noting that on a sampled page of 30 newest stories, ten were already dead, some after only five minutes. The post questions whether automated systems or rapid downvoting by established users are responsible, given that manual review at that speed seems implausible. The discussion touches on Hacker News moderation mechanics, including the role of user flags and algorithmic filters in determining submission visibility.

stories.title: Show HN: I hated how much my 12-year-old played Roblox, so we built our own FPS
stories.paragraph: A parent shared a project in which he and his 12-year-old child built a custom first-person shooter game together as an alternative to the child's heavy use of Roblox, specifically the game Rivals. Rather than restricting screen time through blocking or timers, the father chose to redirect the child's interest toward building a game, using the project as a vehicle for learning. The result, shared at cooked.house, illustrates an approach to parental mediation of gaming that emphasizes creation over consumption.

stories.title: Delta flight hit by firework while landing at Midway Airport on Fourth of July
stories.paragraph: A Delta Airlines flight was struck by a firework while on approach to Chicago's Midway Airport on July 4th, according to NBC Chicago. The aircraft landed safely and no injuries were reported, but the incident prompted an investigation by aviation and law enforcement authorities. Midway Airport's proximity to densely populated residential neighborhoods has historically made it more susceptible to interference from ground-level pyrotechnics during holiday celebrations.
